01a_quotes.title: Generative AI and Firm-level Productivity: Evidence from Startup Funding and Employment Dynamics

study_design: We exploit the release of GitHub Copilot in 2021 as a positive shock to the efficiency of software development.
Our empirical strategy deploys the release of GitHub Copilot as exogenous variation in the efficiency of software development over time.
The empirical setting allows a differences-in-differences (DID) type analysis in which software-developing startups serve as the treatment group and other startups are control group firms.
Formally, we estimate the following fixed effects specification: Yijct = β1(Developer i × P ostt) + βXi + γt + δc + µj + ϵijct , (1)
Applying OLS in our main specifications allows us to use a consistent estimation approach across outcome measures and eases the interpretation of the main coefficients.

The model includes time fixed effects ( γt) that control for business cycle trends or the general investment climate over time, and it controls for country-and industry-specific, time-invariant factors by including country fixed effects ( δc) and industry fixed effects (µj).
Standard errors are clustered at the startup level, with ϵijct representing the error term.
task: We analyze the impact of new GenAI technologies on startups' funding and employment dynamics, i.e., early quantifiable indicators of operational efficiency (e.g., Hsu, 2007; Momtaz, 2021).
We consider startups' time to the second and third funding rounds.
We examine whether the release of GitHub Copilot affected funding patterns in general.
Specifically, we examine whether the post-release period can be associated with changes in investor risk-taking, measured by deal size and syndication.
We examine changes in the number of software developers employed at startups before and after the release of GitHub Copilot.

Hiring skilled developers is one of the first and most resource-intensive steps in scaling software startups, and Copilot may soften this burden: With a reduced time-to-complete programming tasks, fewer software developers would be needed to achieve a given level of output.
population: Our data comprises a novel sample of 21,834 startups that secured their first funding round between Q1 2020 and Q3 2023, including granular information on 225,508 startup employees and founders.

Our sample includes startups headquartered in the US, Canada, and Europe that have successfully raised an initial funding round between Q1 2020 and Q3 2023, a symmetrical time window around the rollout phase of GitHub Copilot.
Consistent with our sampling strategy, startups are relatively young and small when they first receive external funding.
On average, it takes startups about two years after incorporation to secure their first deal, while half of the startups obtain initial funding within 20 months.
The median startup has two founders and eight employees, out of which one is a software developer.
About 22% of startups have at least one founder with a computer science degree.
In our sample, 16.5% of startups are software-developing startups, 71.1% are other software startups, and 12.4% are non-software startups.
arms: Developer i is a dummy that equals one for software-developing startups, as defined in Section 3.3, and zero otherwise.
P ostt is a dummy variable that equals one for startups that raised their initial funding round after the release of GitHub Copilot, and zero otherwise.
As two alternative specifications, we use the public release in June 2022 and the technical release in October 2021.
We define firms as software-developing startups if they operate in sectors in which the main product or service primarily requires them to generate new software solutions.
Other software startups incorporate existing software solutions in their business model, such as apps or online platforms, without explicitly focusing on commercializing self-programmed software.
As a third group, we consider all startups that do not operate in software-related business fields, i.e., non-software startups.
We distinguish three mutually exclusive categories: 1) software-developing startups, 2) other software startups, and 3) non-software startups.
outcome_definitions: We consider the time-to-funding for a startup as the main outcome variable, measuring the duration in continuous months between the incorporation date and the initial deal date.
Subsequent analyses deploy other financing and employment dynamics as dependent variables.

The coefficient of interest, β1, captures the average treatment effect on the treated, i.e., differences in the dynamics between software-developing startups and other startups after the release of GitHub Copilot, relative to the difference before the release.
We examine the effect of GitHub Copilot on startups' time to the second and third funding rounds.
We assess whether the release of GitHub Copilot affected funding patterns in general.
Specifically, we examine whether the post-release period can be associated with changes in investor risk-taking, measured by deal size and syndication.
We estimate Equation (1), using the number of software developers at time of funding as dependent variable.
We use the funding outcomes scaled by the total number of employees as dependent variable.

We find productivity gains in terms of fewer months to initial funding per employee and higher deal sizes per employee after the release of GitHub Copilot, comparing software-developing startups to other startups.
statistical_results: We find that software-developing startups raise their initial funding 19% faster and with 20% fewer employed software developers relative to comparable startups after the release of Copilot.

After the release of GitHub Copilot, software-developing startups experience a four-month decrease in the time-to-funding relative to the control group, implying an economically significant reduction of 19%.
In Column I, the coefficient of the interaction term Developer × P ost is large, negative, and significant at the one percent level.
Its size (-4.793) suggests that software-developing startups experience an average reduction of about five months to initial funding relative to other startups.

Column II shows that this effect is similar but slightly smaller (-3.815) when considering the technical release as treatment event, likely reflecting that the efficiency effects from Copilot require time to unfold.

The coefficient in Column III (3.843) suggests a relative decline of about four months in the time-to-funding for software-developing startups relative to other software startups, equivalent to an 18.5% decrease relative to the mean of 20.768 months.

The coefficient of the interaction term in Column VI (0.294) corresponds to a hazard ratio of 1.341, suggesting that the hazard of obtaining initial funding within the first eight quarters after incorporation increases by 34% for software developing startups relative to other startups after the release of GitHub Copilot.
For the second funding round, the estimates in Table 3 on the DID coefficients are negative, but only significant when considering the public release of GitHub Copilot (Columns I and III).

Comparing software-developing and other software startups, the coefficient size in Column III (-2.312) implies a reduction in the time-to-funding of about two months, equivalent to a relative acceleration by 7.6% compared with the mean of 30.303 months.
This effect is notably smaller than the reduction in the time to the first funding round (18.5%) and does not apply for the technical release of GitHub Copilot (Columns II and IV).
Moreover, the coefficients of the DID estimators are insignificant for all estimates on the third funding round (Columns V-VIII).

The estimates indicate no differential effect on the deal size and syndication of the initial or subsequent investment rounds obtained by software-developing startups after the release of GitHub Copilot relative to other startups (Table A10, Appendix).
The estimates on the "clean" productivity effects are stable across different specifications and subsamples.

In economic terms, the coefficient in Column II implies a lower-bound decrease of 12.7% in the time-to-funding for software-developing startups relative to other software startups after the public release of GitHub Copilot.
Software-developing startups employ a significantly lower average number of programmers at the time of initial funding after the release (2.3) than before (3.1; t-value 6.624).

This pattern does not apply to other software startups (1.9 versus 2.0) or non-software startups (0.9 versus 0.9), for which the number of software developers is not statistically different after the release than before.
The treatment effects are economically meaningful.

For example, the DID estimator in Column III (-0.568) translates to a decrease in the number of software developers by 19.9% of software-developing startups relative to other software startups compared with the mean of 2.856.
Across specifications, the coefficients on the interaction term Developer × Post are negative and statistically significant at the one percent level.

In both cases, only the coefficients in the subsamples of startups whose founders have high experience are statistically significant, while those for startups led by unexprienced founders are insignificant.

Regarding the funding dynamics, the coefficient size implies a four times larger effect for startups led by founders with a computer science degree (34.9%) than for other startups (8.9%), comparing the coefficients in Columns I and II.

Likewise, startups with more experienced founders in terms of previous founding activities exhibit a much larger reduction in the time-to-funding after the release of GitHub Copilot compared to relatively less experienced ones (Columns III and IV).
Here, the coefficient of the DID estimator is about twice as large for startups with experienced founders.
Columns V and VI show that startups with technically experienced founders employ notably fewer software developers after the release of GitHub Copilot relative to other startups.
The coefficient size suggests a five times larger effect for startups with technical co-founders (44.5%) compared to those without (9.1%).
Similarly, startups with serial founders reduce their number of software developers substantially more than those led by first-time founders (Columns VII and VIII).
ambiguity_or_caveats: However, the actual implications of GenAI for individual startup productivity are unclear, a priori.
On the one hand, GenAI may boost productivity by complementing or even substituting human cognitive labor (Noy and Zhang, 2023).
On the other hand, like many other new technologies, GenAI solutions are non-rival and often free-of-charge resources that are available to all market participants (Brynjolfsson and McAfee, 2014).
In other words, they are neither unique, imperfectly imitable, nor controlled by a specific firm.
Hence, they do not yield startups a competitive advantage in the spirit of the resource-based view (Barney, 1991; Barney and Hesterly, 2008), per se.

Importantly, the release of GitHub Copilot is likely to have differential effects across startups: The reduced time-to-completion due to Copilot should be most beneficial for startups whose everyday work activities rely on software development.
Our classification of treated and control firms assumes that the business field of startups represents their propensity to respond to the introduction of GitHub Copilot.
This assumption is supported by statistics on our sample and reflects survey evidence, suggesting that the vast majority of software developers indeed uses GitHub Copilot (GitHub, 2023b).
Still, when exploiting large-scale observational data, it is impossible to directly track individual software developers' programming activities.
As a consequence, we cannot claim with certainty whether and how extensively individual developers in startups use GitHub Copilot's suggestions.
Therefore, future research on the organizational productivity effects of GenAI should also consider using lab and natural experiments, such as randomized control trials.
Our results have natural boundaries to their general applicability across firms.

For example, large incumbent firms usually have different technology adoption patterns than startups since their organizational structure is more complex and hierarchical, and their (perceived) need to increase resource-efficiency may differ.
Similarly, different regulatory and competitive environments may curtail the applicability of our findings.
For instance, as our results suggest, startups in regulated industries such as healthcare and biotech might face challenges in adopting new technologies due to legal hurdles.
Our study represents a first step towards understanding the impact of GenAI on firm-level productivity and, in particular, startup funding and employment dynamics.
The topicality of our analysis limits us from exploring some important performance indicators that will only be measurable over time, such as firm survival, successful exits, or inventive outputs.
By definition, our results provide insights regarding early productivity implications of GenAI.
It will thus be valuable to assess firm-level productivity and competitive dynamics using different outcome measures.
Given GenAI's disruptive potential, we encourage further research to evaluate the long-term implications of the economy-wide adoption.
Consistently, the estimates for startups in these sectors are insignificant (see Table A8, Appendix), reflecting the higher regulatory hurdles to deploy GenAI technologies in their operations.
Crunchbase relies on crowdsourcing as its primary data collection method, which may introduce inaccuracies in the most recent funding data.
As we show, these issues unlikely affect our analysis.
Arguably, all coefficients of the corresponding DID estimators have a negative sign, i.e., irrespective of the founder experience.

To address concerns that the differential effects across the experience levels are only attributed to differences in the precision of the estimates is important to note that also the magnitude of the coefficients is substantially higher for experienced than for inexperienced founders.

01a_quotes.notes: This is an empirical study using a difference-in-differences design exploiting the release of GitHub Copilot as a natural experiment. The paper studies 21,834 startups that raised initial funding between Q1 2020 and Q3 2023. Main outcomes are time-to-funding and number of software developers employed. Results show software-developing startups (treatment group) experienced 19% faster funding and 20% fewer software developers after Copilot release compared to other startups (control group). Effects are stronger for startups with experienced founders. The paper includes extensive robustness checks using Cox proportional hazard models, placebo tests, and analyses of investor attention effects.

01_extraction.title: Generative AI and Firm-level Productivity: Evidence from Startup Funding and Employment Dynamics
01_extraction.study_design_description: The study exploits the release of GitHub Copilot in 2021 as a positive shock to software development efficiency, deploying a differences-in-differences (DID) empirical strategy. Software-developing startups serve as the treatment group and other startups as the control group. The analysis uses a fixed effects specification with OLS estimation, including time fixed effects to control for business cycle trends, and country and industry fixed effects to control for time-invariant factors. Standard errors are clustered at the startup level.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: The study analyzes the impact of GitHub Copilot on startups' funding and employment dynamics as early quantifiable indicators of operational efficiency. Outcomes include time-to-funding for initial, second, and third funding rounds; deal size and syndication patterns; and the number of software developers employed at the time of funding.
01_extraction.population_description: The sample comprises 21,834 startups that secured their first funding round between Q1 2020 and Q3 2023, including 225,508 startup employees and founders. Startups are headquartered in the US, Canada, and Europe. The median startup has two founders and eight employees, with one software developer. About 22% of startups have at least one founder with a computer science degree. In the sample, 16.5% are software-developing startups, 71.1% are other software startups, and 12.4% are non-software startups.

arms.description: Startups that operate in sectors in which the main product or service primarily requires them to generate new software solutions. Defined as Developer = 1 in the DID specification.
Startups that incorporate existing software solutions in their business model, such as apps or online platforms, without explicitly focusing on commercializing self-programmed software.
Startups that do not operate in software-related business fields.

outcomes.outcome_description: The duration in continuous months between the incorporation date and the initial deal date.
The time-to-funding for a startup's second funding round.
The time-to-funding for a startup's third funding round.
The size of funding deals in initial or subsequent investment rounds.
Syndication patterns of initial or subsequent investment rounds, measured as an indicator of investor risk-taking.
The number of software developers employed at the time of initial funding.
Funding outcomes scaled by the total number of employees, measuring productivity gains in terms of months to initial funding per employee.
Deal size scaled by the total number of employees, measuring productivity gains in terms of deal size per employee.

01_extraction.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study exploiting GitHub Copilot's release as a natural experiment. The paper uses two treatment dates: technical release in October 2021 and public release in June 2022. Main findings show software-developing startups (treatment) experienced 19% faster initial funding and 20% fewer software developers compared to other startups (control) after Copilot release. Effects are substantially larger for startups with experienced founders (CS degree, serial founders, technical co-founders). Effects on second funding round are smaller and insignificant for third round. No differential effects on deal size or syndication. The study notes limitations: cannot directly track individual developers' Copilot usage; results may not generalize to large incumbent firms or regulated industries (healthcare, biotech); future research should use lab and natural experiments like RCTs; long-term outcomes (firm survival, exits, inventive outputs) not yet measurable.

02_method.rationale: The study explicitly uses a differences-in-differences (DiD) empirical strategy to exploit the release of GitHub Copilot in 2021 as a positive shock, comparing software-developing startups (treatment) to other startups (control). This is a clear quasi-experimental identification strategy without random assignment.

per_outcome.operationalization: continuous months from incorporation date to initial deal date, measured from company records
continuous months from incorporation to second funding round, measured from company records
continuous months from incorporation to third funding round, measured from company records
dollar amount of funding in investment rounds; reflects investor valuation and capital access, not task speed or quality
indicator of whether multiple investors participate in funding rounds; reflects investor risk-sharing behavior, not task speed or quality
count of software developers employed at time of initial funding; reflects hiring/staffing decisions, not task speed or quality
months to initial funding divided by total employee count, measuring time-efficiency scaled by workforce size
funding amount divided by total employee count; reflects capital efficiency but not task speed or quality

04_onet.rationale: The participants in this study are researchers analyzing 'the impact of GitHub Copilot on startups' funding and employment dynamics' using quantifiable indicators like time-to-funding, deal size, and employment numbers. The core task is analyzing large-scale data (21,834 startups, 225,508 individuals) to identify patterns and relationships. This is fundamentally a data analysis activity, not the work of the startup founders/employees being studied. The researchers are examining secondary data about organizational outcomes, which maps directly to 'Analyzing Data or Information.'

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a difference-in-differences natural experiment. The 'human-only' condition is software-developing startups in the pre-GitHub Copilot period, and the 'AI' condition is the same type of startups in the post-Copilot period. The treatment is the release of GitHub Copilot (AI coding assistant). The comparison arm is 'other startups' (control group that did not benefit from Copilot).
llm_extracted.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study examining the effect of GitHub Copilot's release on software-developing startups. The primary result shows a 19% reduction in time-to-funding (4-month decrease). The regression coefficient β = -4.793 months is in levels (not log-transformed), representing the DiD estimate. Standard error is not reported in the extraction. The paper reports p < 0.01 significance. The 19% figure appears to be calculated as 4.793/baseline_mean, but the exact baseline mean for the treatment group pre-period is not explicitly provided in the extracted statistics (though Column III mentions a mean of 20.768 months for comparison). Without SE and baseline mean, Python will need to rely on the reported percent change of -19% (negative because time decreased, which is beneficial).
source_quotes: We find that software-developing startups raise their initial funding 19% faster and with 20% fewer employed software developers relative to comparable startups after the release of Copilot.

After the release of GitHub Copilot, software-developing startups experience a four-month decrease in the time-to-funding relative to the control group, implying an economically significant reduction of 19%.

In Column I, the coefficient of the interaction term Developer × Post is large, negative, and significant at the one percent level. Its size (-4.793) suggests that software-developing startups experience an average reduction of about five months to initial funding relative to other startups.

computed.notes: This is a difference-in-differences study examining the effect of GitHub Copilot's release on software-developing startups. The primary result shows a 19% reduction in time-to-funding (4-month decrease). The regression coefficient β = -4.793 months is in levels (not log-transformed), representing the DiD estimate. Standard error is not reported in the extraction. The paper reports p < 0.01 significance. The 19% figure appears to be calculated as 4.793/baseline_mean, but the exact baseline mean for the treatment group pre-period is not explicitly provided in the extracted statistics (though Column III mentions a mean of 20.768 months for comparison). Without SE and baseline mean, Python will need to rely on the reported percent change of -19% (negative because time decreased, which is beneficial). | CI missing; variance not computable
